card.title: Why Glass Is Transparent
card.teaser: Glass is made of sand. Sand is made of rock. Somehow, rock you can see through.
card.coverImagePrompt: A pane of crystal-clear glass held up in a dusty workshop with slanted sunbeams passing straight through it, amber dust motes floating on either side, cinematic, Dutch Golden Age lighting

lessons.title: Why isn't everything transparent?
lessons.body: A brick blocks light. A chunk of wood blocks light. A sheet of aluminum foil blocks light. But a pane of glass, made of sand melted together — the exact kind of rock those other things could easily hide behind — lets light stroll right through. The reason has nothing to do with what glass is made of and everything to do with what its electrons refuse to do.

visual.imagePrompt: A split illustration: a brick wall absorbing sunlight on one side, a glass window letting it pass on the other

lessons.title: Electrons are picky eaters
lessons.body: When a photon hits a material, one of three things can happen: the material's electrons can absorb it, reflect it, or let it pass. Electrons can only absorb photons of very specific energies — the ones that exactly match the gap between their allowed energy levels. If a photon's energy doesn't match any gap, the electron simply can't grab it, and the photon sails through.

keyTerms.definition: A single particle of light, carrying a specific amount of energy
The energy difference between an electron's current state and the next available one

visual.imagePrompt: An energy-level diagram showing photons arriving with various energies, some absorbed and others passing through an electron shell

lessons.title: Visible light is invisible to glass
lessons.body: The electrons in glass have a large band gap — bigger than the energy carried by any visible light photon. Red, orange, yellow, green, blue, and violet photons simply can't budge any electrons in glass. With nothing to absorb or scatter them, they travel straight through. Ultraviolet light has more energy and does match glass's band gap, which is why ordinary window glass blocks most UV and why you can't tan through a window.

visual.imagePrompt: A beam of white light hitting a pane of glass, the visible rainbow passing through while a ghosted ultraviolet portion gets absorbed at the surface

lessons.title: And metals are the opposite
lessons.body: Metals look shiny because their outer electrons are free to slosh around — they can absorb and re-emit almost any photon that arrives. That's why a metal mirror reflects light and a sheet of aluminum foil is opaque. Tiny variations in band structure explain why copper looks orange, gold looks yellow, and silver looks colorless: their electrons are tuned to re-emit slightly different mixes of visible frequencies.
lessons.funFact: Medieval stained glass is mostly the same transparent silica — the brilliant colors come from adding a pinch of metal oxide (cobalt for blue, gold for red) that shifts the band gap into visible range.

visual.imagePrompt: A gold bar, a silver coin, and a copper cup each reflecting sunlight with their distinctive tints, against a dark background

lessons.title: Transparency is a negotiation
lessons.body: The weirdest part is that 'see-through' isn't about a material being empty or thin — it's about a mismatch. Glass is dense, solid, and full of atoms; it just happens to be tuned to ignore exactly the wavelengths our eyes evolved to use. If our eyes saw in ultraviolet, every window in the world would look as opaque as a wall.

quiz.question: What determines whether a material absorbs a given photon?
options: Whether the photon's energy matches an available electron transition
quiz.explanation: Electrons only absorb photons whose energy matches the gap between allowed states. If nothing matches, the photon passes through.

quiz.question: Why does ordinary window glass block most ultraviolet light?
options: UV light reflects off glass surfaces
Glass heats up and re-emits UV as infrared
UV photons carry enough energy to match glass's band gap and be absorbed
UV light scatters in all directions inside glass
quiz.explanation: UV photons are energetic enough to excite electrons across the band gap in glass, so they get absorbed rather than passing through.

quiz.question: Why are metals opaque and reflective instead of transparent?
options: They are too dense for photons to enter
Their free electrons absorb and re-emit almost any visible photon
They contain no gaps between atoms
quiz.explanation: Metals have a sea of loosely bound electrons that absorb and instantly re-emit photons of nearly any visible wavelength — producing reflection.

sources.title: The Feynman Lectures on Physics, Vol. I, Ch. 31
